problem text: We flip a fair coin 9 times and aim to find the probability of getting exactly 4 heads among all tosses.
problem solution: Formal Proof: We model each coin toss as an independent Bernoulli trial with success probability $$\frac{1}{2}$$. The total number of ways to choose which 4 out of the 9 tosses will come up heads is given by the binomial coefficient $$\binom{9}{4}$$. Each specific arrangement of heads and tails has probability $$\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^9$$, since each toss contributes a factor of $$\frac{1}{2}$$ to the probability. Therefore, the probability of exactly 4 heads is $$\binom{9}{4} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^9 = 126 \times \frac{1}{512} = \frac{63}{256}$$.
problem hint: Recall that the binomial distribution formula is $$\binom{n}{k}\,p^k\,(1-p)^{n-k}$$. Here, n=9, k=4, and p=1/2.
